title: Access Modifiers
description: Control who can see your data.

slides.title: Public by Default 🌐
slides.content:
In JS and standard TS, every property you declare in a Class is `public`. This means any outside code can read it or blindly modify it (`player.score = -999;`).

This is highly dangerous for application stability.

slides.title: The 'private' Modifier
slides.content: By placing the `private` keyword in front of a property, you lock it so that it can ONLY be accessed or modified from *inside* the class's own methods.

quizData.question: What is the key benefit of explicitly making properties `private`?

options.text: It hides the data from the database.
It forces other code to interact with the data through controlled methods (like `deposit`), preventing unpredictable destructive modifications globally.
It makes the class execute faster.

quizData.explanation: Protecting internal data (encapsulation) prevents bugs where external code accidentally ruins critical object values.

slides.title: The 'protected' Modifier 🛡️
slides.content:
There is a middle ground modifier called `protected`.

It acts exactly like `private` (external code cannot touch it), BUT it allows subclasses (classes that "inherit" from this one) to still access the property internally.

slides.title: Parameter Properties ⚡
slides.content: TypeScript offers a massive shortcut! Instead of declaring properties and setting them in the constructor separately, simply put `public` or `private` *inside* the constructor parameters: `constructor(private name: string) {}` does exactly the same thing instantly!
